papers.title: Runaway and Hypervelocity Stars from Compact Object Encounters in Globular Clusters

abstract.text: The dense environments in the cores of globular clusters (GCs) facilitate many strong dynamical encounters among stellar objects. These encounters have been shown capable of ejecting stars from the host GC, whereupon they become runaway stars, or hypervelocity stars if unbound to the galactic potential. We study high speed stellar ejecta originating from GCs by using Monte Carlo N-body models, in particular focusing on binary-single encounters involving compact objects. We pair our model-discriminated populations with observational catalogs of Milky Way GCs to compose a present-day galactic population of stellar ejecta. We find that these kinds of encounters can accelerate stars to velocities in excess of 2000 km/s, to speeds beyond the previously predicted limits for ejecta from star-only encounters and in the same regime of galactic center ejections. However, the same ejections can only account for 1.5-20% of the total population of stellar runaways, and only 0.0001-1% of hypervelocity stars, with similar relative rates found for runaway white dwarfs. We also provide credible regions for ejecta from 149 Milky Way GCs, which we hope will be useful as supplementary evidence when pairing runaway stars with origin GCs.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Monte Carlo $N$-body simulations reveal that binary-single dynamical encounters involving compact objects (e.g., white dwarfs, neutron stars, black holes) in globular cluster cores can accelerate stars to velocities exceeding 2000 km s⁻¹—surpassing previous limits from star-only encounters and matching galactic center ejection speeds—yet such events account for only 1.5–20% of runaway stars and 0.0001–1% of hypervelocity stars, indicating that while kinematically plausible, these mechanisms are rare contributors to the observed high-velocity stellar population. The study provides credible parameter distributions for ejecta from 149 Milky Way globular clusters, offering a predictive framework to link observed runaway stars to their likely origin clusters via kinematic and astrophysical constraints.

source_fields.title: Runaway and Hypervelocity Stars from Compact Object Encounters in Globular Clusters

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that leverages the presence of black hole subsystems in globular clusters to enhance dynamical ejection scenarios, providing a robust solution to the problem of identifying the true origins of high-velocity stars that evade the classical Hills mechanism and challenge existing interpretations of galactic runaway dynamics. Discussion \\& Conclusions: Strong binary-single compact object encounters in galactic cluster cores produce runaway stars with velocities exceeding 2000 km/s, primarily in early cluster evolution, and contribute a small fraction (<1%) to present-day high-velocity stars, with ejecta kinematics matching observations at low velocities and suggesting a mixture of mechanisms may better explain the full population. GC dynamics and BSCO ejections: This section describes the dynamics of core collapse in globular clusters, including the role of black hole segregation and binary formation, and examines how variations in cluster properties influence black hole ejections and the subsequent evolution of the cluster. Appendix B Ejecta credible regions: This section presents phase-space credible regions (50% and 90%) for ejecta from globular clusters, derived from HEALPix sky and proper motion histograms, to serve as tools for identifying runaway star memberships and guiding further research. A MW-like population of BSCO ejecta: This section describes the methodology for pairing representative CMC models to Milky Way globular clusters by matching their spatial and structural properties in $r_{\\rm gc}$–[Fe/H] and normalized $\\log M$–$r_c/r_h$ spaces to produce a synthetic MW-like population of BSCO ejecta. Methods: Sampling \\& Integrating BSCO encounters: The paper proposes a Hénon-style $N$-body method (CMC) that models collisional stellar dynamics by simulating strong dynamical encounters between neighboring stars and binaries, using probabilistic encounter sampling and a detailed treatment of binary interactions via the Fewbody code, with physics informed by stellar evolution and tidal effects. Appendix A Velocities in encounter rest frames versus the GC rest frame: Neglecting the transformation from the encounter rest frame to the GC rest frame results in only a modest, at most 30% average speed increase—insufficient to significantly alter the number of ejections.

title: Runaway and Hypervelocity Stars from Compact Object Encounters in Globular Clusters

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents Monte Carlo N-body simulations and theoretical modeling of ejection mechanisms, not observational data. Abstract mentions pairing model populations with observational catalogs but does not present new observational stellar data.
